1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-cech-cohomology-derived-presheaves}
Let $X$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
be an open covering of $U \subset X$.
The {\v C}ech cohomology functors $\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, -)$
are canonically isomorphic as a $\delta$-functor to
the right derived functors of the functor
$$
\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) :
\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)
\longrightarrow
\text{Mod}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}.
$$
Moreover, there is a functorial quasi-isomorphism
$$
\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
\longrightarrow
R\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
$$
where the right hand side indicates the right derived functor
$$
R\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) :
D^{+}(\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X))
\longrightarrow
D^{+}(\mathcal{O}_X(U))
$$
of the left exact functor $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Note that the category of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
has enough injectives, see
Injectives, Proposition \ref{injectives-proposition-presheaves-modules}.
Note that $\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)$ is a left exact functor
from the category of presheaves of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
to the category of $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-modules.
Hence the derived functor and the right derived functor exist, see
Derived Categories, Section \ref{derived-section-right-derived-functor}.

\medskip\noindent
Let $\mathcal{I}$ be an injective presheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
In this case the functor $\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(-, \mathcal{I})$
is exact on $\textit{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$. By
Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} we have
$$
\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet, \mathcal{I})
=
\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}).
$$
By Lemma \ref{lemma-homology-complex} we have that $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ is
quasi-isomorphic to $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}[0]$. Hence by
the exactness of Hom into $\mathcal{I}$ mentioned above we see
that $\check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for all
$i > 0$. Thus the $\delta$-functor $(\check{H}^n, \delta)$
(see Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-delta-functor-presheaves})
satisfies the assumptions of
Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-efface-implies-universal},
and hence is a universal $\delta$-functor.

\medskip\noindent
By
Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}
also the sequence $R^i\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -)$
forms a universal $\delta$-functor. By the uniqueness of universal
$\delta$-functors, see
Homology, Lemma \ref{homology-lemma-uniqueness-universal-delta-functor}
we conclude that
$R^i\check{H}^0(\mathcal{U}, -) = \check{H}^i(\mathcal{U}, -)$.
This is enough for most applications
and the reader is suggested to skip the rest of the proof.

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-homology-complex}
Let $X$ be a ringed space.
Let $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an open covering.
Let $\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{O}_X$
be the image presheaf of the map
$\bigoplus j_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_i} \to \mathcal{O}_X$.
The chain complex $K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet$ of presheaves
of Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-map-into} above has homology presheaves
$$
H_i(K(\mathcal{U})_\bullet) =
\left\{
\begin{matrix}
0 & \text{if} & i \not = 0 \\
\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U} & \text{if} & i = 0
\end{matrix}
\right.
$$
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Consider the extended complex $K^{ext}_\bullet$ one gets by putting
$\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}$ in degree $-1$ with the obvious map
$K(\mathcal{U})_0 =
\bigoplus_{i_0} (j_{i_0})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0}} \to
\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}$.
It suffices to show that taking sections of this extended complex over
any open $W \subset X$ leads to an acyclic complex.
In fact, we claim that for every $W \subset X$ the complex
$K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$ is homotopy equivalent to the zero complex.
Write $I = I_1 \amalg I_2$ where $W \subset U_i$ if and only
if $i \in I_1$.

\medskip\noindent
If $I_1 = \emptyset$, then the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W) = 0$ so there is
nothing to prove.

\medskip\noindent
If $I_1 \not = \emptyset$, then
$\mathcal{O}_\mathcal{U}(W) = \mathcal{O}_X(W)$
and
$$
K^{ext}_p(W) =
\bigoplus\nolimits_{i_0 \ldots i_p \in I_1} \mathcal{O}_X(W).
$$
This is true because of the simple description of the presheaves
$(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_{p!}\mathcal{O}_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}$.
Moreover, the differential of the complex $K^{ext}_\bullet(W)$
is given by
$$
d(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} =
\sum\nolimits_{j = 0, \ldots, p + 1} \sum\nolimits_{i \in I_1}
(-1)^j s_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}.
$$
The sum is finite as the element $s$ has finite support.
Fix an element $i_{\text{fix}} \in I_1$. Define a map
$$
h : K^{ext}_p(W) \longrightarrow K^{ext}_{p + 1}(W)
$$
by the rule
$$
h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} =
\left\{
\begin{matrix}
0 & \text{if} & i_0 \not = i_{\text{fix}} \\
s_{i_1 \ldots i_{p + 1}} & \text{if} & i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}
\end{matrix}
\right.
$$
We will use the shorthand
$h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p + 1}} = (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_p}$
for this. Then we compute
\begin{eqnarray*}
& & (dh + hd)(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \\
& = &
\sum_j \sum_{i \in I_1} (-1)^j h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}
+
(i = i_0) d(s)_{i_1 \ldots i_p} \\
& = &
s_{i_0 \ldots i_p} +
\sum_{j \geq 1}\sum_{i \in I_1}
(-1)^j (i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) s_{i_1 \ldots i_{j - 1} i i_j \ldots i_p}
+
(i_0 = i_{\text{fix}}) d(s)_{i_1 \ldots i_p}
\end{eqnarray*}
which is equal to $s_{i_0 \ldots i_p}$ as desired.
\end{proof}
